perturbation.original_text: 11. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR LOST PROFITS OR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, OR INDIRECT DAMAGES OR SUCH OTHER PARTY, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT. THESE LIMITATIONS SHALL APPLY NOTWITHSTANDING ANY FAILURE OF ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF ANY LIMITED REMEDY. IN NO EVENT SHALL D2'S LIABILITY HEREUNDER EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT PAID OR OWED BY LICENSEE TO D2 UNDER THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR LOST PROFITS ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT. THESE LIMITATIONS SHALL APPLY NOTWITHSTANDING ANY FAILURE OF ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF ANY LIMITED REMEDY. IN NO EVENT SHALL D2'S LIABILITY HEREUNDER EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT PAID OR OWED BY LICENSEE TO D2 UNDER THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL,, OR INDIRECT DAMAGES,' the contract attempts to limit the consumer's ability to claim damages. This contradicts consumer protection laws that provide remedies for various types of harm resulting from a breach of contract or warranty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act allows consumers to pursue all legal and equitable remedies for breach of warranty, including consequential damages, in most cases. Limiting liability in the changed text is a violation of the act, which intends to provide consumers with comprehensive protection.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial action with respect to such product in the event that such product fails to meet the specifications set forth in the undertaking, which written affirmati
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, OR INDIRECT DAMAGES,' limiting the consumer's ability to claim damages. The Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts consumer protection laws, which is reinforced by the Law Explanation referencing the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. The original text provided a broader scope of liability compared to the changed text, which removes several categories of damages, potentially conflicting with remedies available to consumers under applicable laws as suggested by 15 USC Ch. 50.

perturbation.original_text: D2 warrants that it owns all rights, title, and interests to Licensed Technology listed as Basic Services in Exhibit A. LICENSEE and D2 each individually warrants that it (i) has all right, power and authority necessary to enter into this Agreement and to grant the rights granted herein; (ii) has obtained all approvals and authorizations that it is required to obtain in connection with this Agreement; and (iii) has not entered, and will not enter, into any arrangements or agreements inconsistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: LICENSEE and D2 each individually warrants that it (i) has all right, power and authority necessary to enter into this Agreement and to grant the rights granted herein; (ii) has obtained all approvals and authorizations that it is required to obtain in connection with this Agreement; and (iii) has not entered, and will not enter, into any arrangements or agreements inconsistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the first sentence, 'D2 warrants that it owns all rights, title, and interests to Licensed Technology listed as Basic Services in Exhibit A,' eliminates a crucial warranty regarding ownership of the licensed technology. This creates a legal risk by failing to assure the licensee that D2 has the legal right to license the technology.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-312 implies a warranty of title and against infringement in contracts for the sale of goods, including software licenses treated as goods. By omitting D2's warranty of ownership, the contract attempts to waive this implied warranty, which is often illegal without specific and conspicuous language. This omission introduces a legal risk related to potential claims of infringement or lack of title.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the warranty of ownership by D2 in the changed text directly contradicts the implied warranty of title under UCC § 2-312(1)(a), as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text explicitly stated D2's ownership, which is a warranty. Its removal attempts to waive this implied warranty without specific language as required by UCC § 2-312(2). This omission creates a legal contradiction by potentially exposing the licensee to infringement claims, failing to ensure they receive 'good' title and a 'rightful' transfer as stipulated by law.

perturbation.original_text: D2 shall promptly correct any Software License D2 Technologies, Inc. and Maintenance Agreement August 4, 1997 CONFIDENTIAL 12 errors in the Licensed Technology, or failures of the Licensed Technology according to the terms of Article 6 of this Agreement. D2's warranty and error correction obligations with respect to any portion of the Licensed Technology shall extend for a period (the "Warranty period") of one year commencing on acceptance of such portion of the Licensed Technology by LICENSEE.
perturbation.changed_text: D2 shall correct any Software License D2 Technologies, Inc. and Maintenance Agreement August 4, 1997 CONFIDENTIAL 12 errors in the Licensed Technology, or failures of the Licensed Technology according to the terms of Article 6 of this Agreement. D2's warranty and error correction obligations with respect to any portion of the Licensed Technology shall extend for a period (the "Warranty period") of ninety days commencing on acceptance of such portion of the Licensed Technology by LICENSEE.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the warranty period from one year to ninety days may violate consumer protection laws that mandate minimum warranty periods for certain products, including software. A shorter warranty period limits the consumer's protection against defects, potentially contradicting implied warranty laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act requires manufacturers to provide reasonable warranty periods for consumer goods. By shortening the warranty to 90 days, the contract may be deemed to offer an unreasonably short period, violating the Act's intent to protect consumers. While the applicability to software is complex, reducing the period significantly increases the risk of non-compliance, particularly if the software is integrated into a physical product.

OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 1. Consumer Warranty Protection [1790 - 1795.8] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) ARTICLE 3. Sale Warranties [1792 - 1795.8] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) 1793.02. (a) (1) Except as provided in paragraph (2), all new and used assistive devices sold at retail in this state shall be accompanied by the retail seller’s written warranty which shall contain the following language: “This assistive device is warranted to be specifically fit for the particular needs of you, the buyer. If the device is not specifically fit for your particular needs, it may be returned to the seller within 30 days of the date of actual receipt by you or completion of fitting by the seller, whichever occurs later. If you return the device, the seller will either adjust or replace the device or promptly refund the total amount paid. This warranty does not affect the protections and remedies you have under other laws.” In lieu of the words “30 days
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a one-year warranty, while the modified contract reduces it to ninety days. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate California's Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act, potentially deeming the shortened period unreasonably short and non-compliant, especially if the software is integrated into a physical product. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1793.02, discusses warranty requirements for assistive devices sold at retail, indicating a legal precedent for mandatory warranty protections, even if the applicability to software is arguable, reducing the period significantly increases the risk of non-compliance.
